mappings.rationaleAI: A comprehensive survey of human development from hunter-gatherer origins to the present, drawing on archaeology, anthropology, linguistics, and genetics, represents a type of historical writing covering universal human experience (A.2) and employing diverse sources for historical writing (B.1).
The Renaissance's recovery of classical antiquity, humanism, and Petrarch's rediscovery of classical texts directly connects to Renaissance historiography (A.1.c.i).

Sociology's methods including comparative historical study and its theoretical traditions provide auxiliary frameworks for historical investigation, functioning as a discipline supporting historical interpretation alongside anthropology and other social sciences (B.2).
Cicero's speeches and letters as primary sources for the late Roman Republic, and his prose style that influenced later historians, connect to classical Greek and Roman historiography (A.1.a.ii).

The history of science as a discipline tracing empirical inquiry across civilizations represents a type of historical writing organized by theme (A.2), with its methodology involving philosophy and sociology of science reflecting factors involved in writing history (A.3).
Social science disciplines provide auxiliary methods for historical investigation (B.2), including empirical and interpretive approaches that inform historical research methodology.

The post-classical period (c. 500-1500 CE) directly overlaps with medieval historiography including Western Christian (A.1.b.i) and Byzantine (A.1.b.ii) traditions, and as a global rather than Eurocentric framing it connects to non-Western historiographical traditions (A.1.d).

Marx's historical materialism and dialectical method constitute a speculative philosophy of history offering a pattern-based explanation of historical events through class struggle and economic determinism (C.2), directly relevant to 19th-and 20th-century historiography (A.1.c.iv).
Freud's psychoanalytic framework is explicitly listed as an auxiliary discipline for ascertaining and interpreting historical sources (B.2.n).

The intensive intellectual production of the medieval Islamic world, including philosophical and scientific scholarship in Baghdad, Cordoba, and Cairo, connects to Islamic historiography as a non-Western historiographical tradition (A.1.d.i).
The Enlightenment's emphasis on reason and empirical inquiry directly shaped 18th-century historiography (A.1.c.iii), with encyclopedias and the print trade disseminating historical knowledge.

The academic discipline of history, covering source criticism, schools of thought (positivism, Annales, Marxism, postmodernism), methodological variants, and debates over objectivity, maps comprehensively across historiography (A), historical investigation and research (B), and philosophy of history (C).

The period from invention of writing through late antiquity connects to Near Eastern, classical, and early Christian historiography (A.1.a). The three-age system and reliance on written records highlight sources for historical writing (B.1) and auxiliary disciplines including archaeology (B.2.b) and chronology (B.2.d).

Ibn Khaldun's Muqaddimah introduced systematic historiographical method and cyclical theories of dynastic rise and fall, representing Islamic historiography (A.1.d.i) and a speculative philosophy of history explaining the pattern of historical events (C.2).

Machiavelli's Florentine Histories and empirical analysis of political power represent Renaissance historiography (A.1.c.i) and illustrate the influence of the author's background and vantage point on historical writing (A.3).

Kant's critical philosophy and Enlightenment-era thought contributed to 18th-century historiography (A.1.c.iii) and his ideas about perpetual peace and teleological judgment connect to philosophical speculation about the historical process (C).

Voltaire's Essay on Universal History and The Age of Louis XIV represent landmark works of Enlightenment historiography (A.1.c.iii), and his satirical, rationalist perspective illustrates the background and purpose of the historian (A.3).

European history's conventional periodization from classical antiquity through the Middle Ages to the modern era tracks the development of historiography from classical Greek and Roman (A.1.a.ii) through medieval (A.1.b) to Renaissance and modern (A.1.c), representing a major type of historical writing by geography (A.2).

The history of religion as a discipline examining religious texts, institutions, and beliefs cross-culturally connects to early Christian historiography (A.1.a.iii) and constitutes a thematic type of historical writing (A.2).
Ancient Greek civilization, with its philosophical and literary achievements, constitutes the origin of classical historiography in the Western tradition (A.1.a.ii).
